definition: Immigration is the highest-variance demographic variable on short horizons, with the US immigration swing (±2M persons/year) exceeding cumulative natural increase (~1M/year) by 2x. The OECD-wide simultaneous shift toward restriction is structural, not a single country's policy cycle, though policy can reverse rapidly.
evidence: US immigration swing (±2M persons/year) exceeds cumulative natural increase (~1M/year) by 2x
CBO estimates ~0.3-0.4pp GDP growth contribution from immigration surge
OECD-wide simultaneous restriction: US, Canada, Australia, UK, Italy, France, Netherlands, Sweden all tightening
Base rate: only 3/6 democracies sustained high immigration >10 years
Multiple distinct transmission channels: agricultural labor, housing formation/RMBS pipeline, leveraged borrower margins, potential GDP growth
open_questions: Is the OECD immigration restrictionist turn cyclical (18-36 months) or structural (10+ years)?
What is the empirical macro transmission to rates/inflation? Confidence interval on r* effect spans zero
Can policy reverse rapidly enough to offset labor supply contraction?
